evidence_quotes.quote: Firstly, the issue of video game publishers destroying games that consumers have already bought, without giving money back.
Said group demanded that the payment processors effectively censor thousands upon thousands of games because they perceive them as immoral.

paragraphs.text: Good day. I would like to bring attention to two more issues that I think fall in the scope of proposed legislation. Firstly, the issue of video game publishers destroying games that consumers have already bought, without giving money back. If I bought a game, I would expect to be entitled to a copy of it until the day I die. I am not suggesting companies have to support games indefinitely, merely that they have to leave them in a functional state so that consumers can use and enjoy them in the future. Hundreds, if not thousands of games have already been effectively destroyed. Video games have one of the worst records when it comes to preservation, on par with extremely old records from the second half of the 19th century and the beginning of the 20th century. This can be expanded to most if not all digital multimedia including films, songs, various audio files, books, texts, digital art, 3D art, animations, videos etc. I personally believe that all art should be preserved for future generations, including pieces that I might personally dislike, find immoral etc. Secondly, as many of my fellow citizens have recently pointed out, there has been an incident with a lobby group Collective Shout e-mailing and calling payment processors such as Visa and MasterCard. Said group demanded that the payment processors effectively censor thousands upon thousands of games because they perceive them as immoral. The games were legal, otherwise digital storefronts such as Steam or Itch.io would not have sold them on their web stores. The payment processors complied due to fear of negative press and fear it would hurt their brand image, and consequently threatened Steam and Itch.io with denial of their services (processing payments). The storefronts, faced with extreme financial loss, complied in turn, therefore granting Collective Shout a win. Payment processors should not have this much power and control over citizens money and decisions. In short, I do not think neither Collective Shout nor Visa/MasterCard should decide how I or any other citizen spends their money (legally, of course). One solution to this would be to have a public EU-owned payment processor alternative that wouldn't be susceptible to such censorship (if it was legislated properly), but I digress. I politely ask the initiative to include these issues in the Digital fairness Act. Kind regards, A concerned citizen.
